ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Indian-style palak soup; A00GJ (vegetable soup) used as closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for palak soup; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: may contain gluten (if gram flour used)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Palak Soup is approximately 250 ml (one bowl), equivalent to 250 g, providing roughly 112 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Palak Soup are adapted from ICMR-NIN spinach and Indian vegetable soup composition data. Palak (spinach) nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods used as primary anchor; values adjusted for typical soup preparation with added aromatics, gram flour or cream, and dilution. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (spinach, cooked, boiled) used as secondary cross-reference for micronutrient plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style Palak Soup. GI estimated at 15 based on the GI of cooked spinach and vegetable-based pureed soups from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Spinach is a very low-GI vegetable (GI ~15); the soup preparation with minimal starch addition does not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 11.25) / 100 ≈ 1.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (150 mg/100 g); high potassium (280 mg/100 g) and magnesium (45 mg/100 g) support vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Palak Soup is generally suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its high potassium-to-sodium ratio, which supports blood pressure regulation; however, added salt during preparation should be minimised to keep sodium intake within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load (~1.7 per serving); high dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) slows glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Palak Soup is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its very low GI and high fibre content result in minimal postprandial blood glucose elevation; it can be included freely as a starter or light meal component within a balanced diabetic diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), high vitamin K (350 mcg/100 g), folate (120 mcg/100 g), and potassium support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Palak Soup is cardioprotective due to its low saturated fat content, high folate (which reduces homocysteine), and abundant potassium and magnesium; individuals on warfarin should note the very high vitamin K content (350 mcg/100 g) and maintain consistent intake to avoid INR fluctuations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidants and Vitamin K may reduce inflammation; high oxalate content warrants monitoring
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Spinach is rich in antioxidants and Vitamin K which may help reduce inflammatory markers; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are also prone to oxalate-related issues should monitor their personal response to regular spinach consumption.

kidney_stones.key_factor: Spinach is very high in oxalates
kidney_stones.guidance: Individuals with a history of calcium oxalate kidney stones should limit or avoid Palak Soup, as spinach is one of the highest dietary sources of oxalates and regular consumption may increase urinary oxalate excretion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Palak Soup is a nutritious, vibrant green Indian-style spinach soup made by blanching fresh spinach leaves and pureeing them into a smooth base, which is then simmered with aromatic ingredients including garlic, ginger, and onions. The soup is lightly seasoned with cumin and black pepper, and may be thickened with a small amount of gram flour (besan) or cream to achieve a velvety consistency. It is a popular healthy starter across India, served at lunch, dinner, or as a light snack. The flavour profile is earthy, mildly spiced, and slightly creamy, with a characteristic deep green colour from the spinach. Nutritionally, Palak Soup is exceptionally rich in Vitamin A (450 mcg RAE/100 g), Vitamin K (350 mcg/100 g), folate (120 mcg DFE/100 g), iron (2.5 mg/100 g), calcium (120 mg/100 g), and magnesium (45 mg/100 g), making it one of the most micronutrient-dense Indian soups. It is low in calories (45 kcal/100 g), low in fat (2.5 g/100 g), and has a very low glycemic index (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The high oxalate content of spinach is a caution for individuals prone to calcium oxalate kidney stones. A standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing around 112 kcal. The dish is vegetarian and can be made vegan by omitting cream.
common_user_queries: Is palak soup good for diabetes?
How many calories in palak soup?
What are the benefits of palak soup?
Is palak soup good for high blood pressure?
Can I eat palak soup if I have kidney stones?
What is the GI of spinach soup?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN spinach and Indian vegetable soup composition data rather than a direct exact match for Palak Soup as a prepared dish. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as secondary cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked spinach GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. No single approved source provides a complete nutrient profile for this exact preparation.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — Palak Soup is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard preparation; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods, confirmed by ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — spinach and typical soup aromatics contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (spinach, cooked). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in standard preparation. 4. Saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7 g/100 g) are estimated from ICMR-NIN spinach fatty acid data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (spinach, cooked, boiled, drained) as no direct fatty acid breakdown for Palak Soup is available in approved sources. 5. Cholesterol (2.0 mg/100 g) is estimated assuming a small amount of cream is used; if prepared without cream, cholesterol is 0. 6. Water_g (88.0 g/100 g) is estimated from the soup's liquid nature and spinach water content per USDA FoodData Central. 7. Portions in the input JSON contained three identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to one serving of 250 ml / 250 g. 8. The input source cited ICMR-NIN as the primary source; this is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes and has been retained. 9. Vitamin K value (350 mcg/100 g) is notably high and clinically relevant for individuals on anticoagulant therapy (warfarin); this is consistent with spinach being one of the highest dietary sources of Vitamin K, as documented in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.
